text: said . Warlord’s finger stilled, and he frowned. “Chaos birthed the world we’re forced to exist within. It’s a world that needs to be tamed. Order brings prosperity, which benefits us all. Even the meatbags .” “What does that have to do with me? Am I an agent of chaos, a thing to be tamed ?” “No. You’re capable. Never had a walker come through who brought in so much scrap so regularly. You come back damaged, sometimes, but you always come back.” Warlord turned his gaze to Ronin; his optics were the gray of old steel. “How many have you ended out there? How many have you left behind to be claimed by the Dust ?” Ronin’s hands twitched on his lap. The faces of every human and bot he’d destroyed were preserved in his memory, frozen eternally in the instant before death or deactivation. He would carry them until his own end, always with perfect clarity. It was the memories he couldn’t place — the faces that drifted up from his damaged core, the ones from before the Black Out — that concerned him most . There were hundreds more of those . “You’re a danger, dustwalker. Not because you’ve killed. Most of us have done that.” Warlord’s finger resumed tapping, slowly but forcefully, no longer with any regard to the rhythm. “You’re a danger because you disregard the rules. The rules are what set this place apart, what raised it above the rest. Otherwise, this placed would be the same as all the other ruins you’ve picked through. Another gravestone in a worldwide cemetery .” “I should have sheltered in the Dust tonight .” “No.” Warlord slammed his fist on the table and leaned forward, scarred face still impassive despite the intensity in his eyes. Several patrons glanced over, looking away quickly. “You should have shown some fucking respect .” “Your guards haven’t earned my respect yet .” “You shelter here in private quarters because I allow it. I offer that to all bots, because we’re the ones who will rebuild this world and make it whole again. All I ask is that my rules are followed. They’re not difficult. All the bots that wear my mark have the authority to enforce those rules. Disrespecting them is disrespecting me .” Ronin forced his jaw closed and temporarily cut power to his vocal modulator to keep any words from escaping. Dozens of bots wore Warlord’s mark. Ronin would never make it out of the Market if Warlord commanded it . “You took some bullets out there,” Warlord said after several seconds, easing back into his chair and swinging his optics back to the dancers. “Behave yourself, so you don’t collect a few more on your way to the Clinic .” Ronin pressed a bare metal hand to the table and pushed himself up. A strange sensation skittered over his palm, and his processors, unbidden, ran a simulation — if he unslung his rifle, he could empty the magazine into Warlord’s chest. Power cell, memory banks, CPU…at this range, it would all be annihilated. The simulation ended with Ronin torn apart by the behemoth at the front door. There weren’t enough bullets in Cheyenne to stop Comp. Still, it was strangely satisfying . He shoved his hands into his coat pockets. Zeke should nearly be done with his assessment, by now. With a last glance at the stage, Ronin left Kitty’s. He couldn’t determine whether the yellow unit for admission had been wasted . Chapter Four F or a long while, Lara lay awake. The shock and fear of having a bot enter her home lingered for hours after its departure. Every sound — every creaking board, every distant coyote howl, every clang of the chimes — could be sign of the bot’s return . Exhaustion eventually claimed her . She awoke from a fitful sleep, more tired than before, to find the gray light of dawn seeping through the edges of the doorway . Lara sat up on her pallet, plucking the canteen off the floor to study it in the soft glow of the lantern. Her payment . Payment for a bot spying on her. Payment for a bot violating her home. Her arm tensed, and for a moment she struggled, longing to throw the container against the wall, to batter it into a dented, useless clump of metal . But the canteen was hers, now — a valuable item, regardless of its source — and the bot was gone. No different than the pitcher she’d given Gary and Kate. Despite her anger, she couldn’t allow herself to throw away something useful . She wanted nothing more to do with bots, but she couldn’t change the events of last night. What was done, was done . Lowering the canteen into her lap, she brushed her fingers over its worn canvas covering. All the bot had asked of her was a dance. No threats, no demands. It hadn’t even reached for its weapon. Not that it needed one … She’d been surprised to see someone at her door. Her eyes fell on his face, first, and she couldn’t ignore how attractive he was. His strong jaw, intense green eyes…but when the lantern light reflected off the metal of his hand, cold fear flooded her veins. How could she forget that night, not long ago, when she learned what bots were truly capable of . Lara shook her head, thrusting the thoughts away. She tossed the canteen onto the blankets and pulled her boots out from beneath the crate, pausing to examine them. To call them worn would be an understatement. The threads were unraveling, the leather thin and showing signs of tearing . She should have traded with Cole while she had items to spare; he knew how to piece footwear back together. After tugging them on, she knelt beside the pallet and reached under it to retrieve her knife. She stood and strapped it to her thigh.
